---
title: "Exam 3: Shiny"
format: html
author: "David Rios"
---

## Introduction

This is a tutorial of how to make interactive Visualizations in R.
In this tutorial, we are going to be using Shiny which is an R package that makes it easy to build interactive web applications (apps) straight from R.

## Shiny and Interactive Dashboards

Shiny is an R package that allows you to turn static analysis into interactive dashboards and web apps.
Unlike a single plot, Shiny apps enable users to **engage with the data**—filtering, adjusting parameters, and exploring patterns in real time.
This makes Shiny especially powerful when your goal is not just to present results, but to **let users discover insights themselves**.

At its core, every Shiny app has three components:

-   **UI (User Interface):** controls the layout and inputs (e.g., sliders, dropdowns)\
-   **Server:** contains the logic and computations\
-   **`shinyApp()`**: connects UI and server to run the app

What makes Shiny unique is **reactivity**.
Instead of rerunning your entire script, Shiny only updates the parts of the app that depend on user input.
For example, in the “Hello Shiny” app, changing the number of bins automatically updates the histogram—no manual rerun required.

## Connecting Shiny to Dashboard Tradeoffs

This is where Shiny connects directly to the **design tradeoffs** discussed earlier:

-   **Interaction increases:** Shiny enables filters, sliders, and dynamic visuals, making dashboards more exploratory\
-   **Number of pages can decrease:** one app can contain multiple views instead of many static plots\
-   **Screenspace becomes constrained:** layouts must be carefully designed to avoid clutter\
-   **Abstraction becomes critical:** dashboards often start with summaries and allow drill-downs

However, these benefits come at a cost:

-   More **complexity in code and design**\
-   Higher **development time**\
-   Risk of **over-interaction**, where users get lost instead of informed

## Takeaway

Use Shiny when your audience needs to *interact* with the data.
If your goal is simply to communicate one clear message, a well-designed static visualization is often the better choice.

## Code Appendix

```{r eval=FALSE}
install.packages("shiny")

# Here is the ui object for the Hello Shiny example.

library(shiny)
library(bslib)

# Define UI for app that draws a histogram ----
ui <- page_sidebar(
  # App title ----
  title = "Hello Shiny!",
  # Sidebar panel for inputs ----
  sidebar = sidebar(
    # Input: Slider for the number of bins ----
    sliderInput(
      inputId = "bins",
      label = "Number of bins:",
      min = 1,
      max = 50,
      value = 30
    )
  ),
  # Output: Histogram ----
  plotOutput(outputId = "distPlot")
)

# Here is the server function for the Hello Shiny example.
# Define server logic required to draw a histogram ----
server <- function(input, output) {

  # Histogram of the Old Faithful Geyser Data ----
  # with requested number of bins
  # This expression that generates a histogram is wrapped in a call
  # to renderPlot to indicate that:
  #
  # 1. It is "reactive" and therefore should be automatically
  #    re-executed when inputs (input$bins) change
  # 2. Its output type is a plot
  output$distPlot <- renderPlot({

    x    <- faithful$waiting
    bins <- seq(min(x), max(x), length.out = input$bins + 1)

    hist(x, breaks = bins, col = "#007bc2", border = "white",
         xlab = "Waiting time to next eruption (in mins)",
         main = "Histogram of waiting times")

    })

}
```
